title: Electoral Pluralism Index
subtitle: Data by the [Economist Intelligence Unit](#dod:economist-intelligence-unit). Expert estimates of the extent to which all citizens can choose their political leaders in free and fair elections. Higher values indicate more pluralism.
caption:
Electoral Pluralism Index, 2024

Data by the Economist Intelligence Unit. Expert estimates of the extent to which all citizens can choose their political leaders in free and fair elections. Higher values indicate more pluralism.

This choropleth map displays the Electoral Pluralism Index for 2024, using a continuous color scale from deep red (score of 1) to dark blue (score of 10) to represent the extent to which citizens can choose political leaders in free and fair elections. The highest values, falling in the 8 to 10 range, are visible across North America, including Canada and the United States, as well as throughout Western and Northern Europe, Australia, and New Zealand. In South America, countries like Brazil and Argentina appear in the mid-range bands, while Venezuela is colored in the lowest red band. Across Africa, the distribution is mixed; South Africa and Namibia show higher scores in the blue spectrum, whereas a large contiguous block of nations in North and Central Africa, including Sudan and Chad, are shaded in the lowest red categories. Similarly, the Middle East and Central Asia are dominated by red and orange hues, indicating low pluralism scores, with Russia and China also appearing in the deep red band. India is visible in a light blue shade, placing it in a middle category. Greenland and several other territories are marked with diagonal hatching to indicate no data is available. The data shows a clear geographic clustering where high pluralism is concentrated in the Americas and Western Europe, while low pluralism is prevalent across much of Asia, the Middle East, and Africa.
